narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared your undergraduate GPA of 2.90, LSAT score of 34, middle family income bracket, and plans to attend full-time, along with your school deciles from Year 1 and Year 3 both sitting at the lowest 10th percentile. The admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on patterns from thousands of past students, estimated a 74.3% chance that you would fail the bar exam—high enough that your application was rejected to protect the school's outcomes. This prediction wasn't random; it hinged on a few key aspects of your profile that stood out the most. The strongest influence pushing toward failure was your Year 3 school decile in the bottom 10%, well below the typical student's mid-range position around the 5th or 6th decile, signaling to the model a significant late-stage academic struggle that often foreshadows bar challenges. Next in importance was your Year 1 decile, also in the bottom 10% compared to averages around the 6th decile, reinforcing concerns about foundational performance from the start. Your LSAT score offered some counterbalance, pulling the prediction slightly toward passing since 34 is reasonably competitive, though still under the dataset average of 36.7. Overall, these academic deciles carried more weight than your LSAT, family background, GPA, or full-time status, as they most strongly mirrored profiles of students who later failed the bar. Compared to typical applicants who pass, your early and late deciles are notably lower, which tipped the scales despite other solid elements like your full-time commitment that aligns with 93% of the dataset. This doesn't define your potential—many overcome similar starts through targeted effort. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it above average, strengthening your recent academic record perhaps through post-bac courses or a master's program to improve those deciles, and gathering strong recommendation letters highlighting your resilience as a Hispanic student navigating these challenges. Reapplying after addressing these gaps could shift the model's view in your favor.
